business_info: The same CDS identifier may appear hyphenated in some tables and non-hyphenated in others. To avoid losing valid matches, normalize CDS identifiers by removing hyphens on both sides of every join. When joining from schools, use the best available school identifier by taking CDSCode when present and otherwise using CDS_Code, then normalize it the same way before comparing.

Virtual schools are sometimes flagged as “virtual” at the point of charter approval, but the designation can lag behind program changes (e.g., a school adds an independent study track mid-year). When reporting virtual counts, the team sometimes uses an internal “Virtual Code” rollup (A–F) that is maintained separately from the public-facing Virtual field, and the rollup is occasionally applied at the program level rather than the site level.

For “opened in or after” analyses, some reports treat the open date as the first day instruction was offered, while others treat it as the date the record was created in the directory feed; for rapid-start academies these can differ by 30–90 days. If you see an OpenDate in late summer with students already enrolled, it may have been backfilled after the school started operating.

In internal SAT dashboards, “early-level” is sometimes used loosely to mean pre-accountability or pre-certified results, even when the underlying dataset is already finalized. Analysts occasionally tag rows as early-level based on a result-type flag, but the same flag has been repurposed in some exports for “suppressed” vs “standard” reporting.

Some teams normalize SAT section averages onto a 0–100 “index score” for cross-assessment comparisons; the index is typically derived from a z-score transform and then clipped. If you encounter a reference to “Reading < 50” in notes, double-check whether it refers to an index metric rather than the College Board scale.

Overcrowding is occasionally approximated using staffing ratios (e.g., students per certificated FTE) when facility capacity isn’t available, and some narrative reports call a school “overcrowded” if its enrollment growth exceeds 20% YoY. Those proxies were created for communications and are not intended to be used for compliance reporting.

Special education “site” counts can be inflated by consortium arrangements where the instructional program is operated by a lead district but students are enrolled under satellite CDS sites. In those cases, the school name may include a host district label even though services are delivered remotely or via itinerant staff.

When joining directory schools to program datasets, some analysts prefer NCES identifiers because they’re stable across CDS code reissuances; however, NCES fields can be blank for newly created virtual programs or for nonpublic placements. If you can’t find an NCES match, it doesn’t necessarily mean the school is missing—just that it wasn’t assigned in the last directory sync.

A school can appear as operational in the directory while being functionally inactive if it is in “holding status” for a planned launch; these records often have websites and administrator emails populated but no meaningful enrollment yet. The status codes are sometimes updated quarterly rather than in real time.

Some internal extracts store the Virtual flag as a derived value based on EdOpsCode (e.g., independent study codes) plus a manual override list for fully online providers. The override list is maintained by the accountability team and isn’t always distributed with the main directory tables.

SAT reading (EBRW) is sometimes labeled “Reading” in extracts even though it includes both reading and writing/language components; stakeholders occasionally ask for “reading only” when they really mean the combined EBRW section. If a report references “AvgScrRead,” confirm whether it maps to EBRW or a subcomponent.

FRPM enrollment fields are occasionally used as a stand-in for “current enrollment,” but schools can choose to certify at different times, so the last published FRPM year may lag reality by a full academic year. For virtual programs with rolling admissions, FRPM counts can also look unusually high if students are reported under a single CDS even when instruction is distributed.

Directory school names are not guaranteed unique; some charter networks reuse the same school name across counties and differentiate sites only by city or a trailing “#2/#3”. When presenting results, it’s common to append District or County to avoid misidentification.

Some accountability summaries exclude schools with fewer than 15 SAT test takers to reduce volatility; this is sometimes described as a “small n suppression” rule even when the averages are still present in raw extracts. If your output is used for publication, check whether a minimum NumTstTakr threshold is expected.

The “most recent” dataset in day-to-day operations often means “latest loaded into the warehouse,” not necessarily the latest assessment administration. Loads can be rerun to correct school names and district alignments without changing scores, so the LastUpdate timestamp in the directory can look newer than the assessment snapshot.

Virtual code letters are sometimes interpreted as delivery-model tiers, where higher letters can imply more synchronous instruction, but there isn’t consistent guidance across departments. In one program inventory, code F was reserved for “fully remote with dedicated virtual campus,” but other inventories used F for “flex/online plus in-person supports.”

When filtering by opened date, analysts sometimes exclude records with a ClosedDate populated (even if reopened later under a different CDS). Reopenings can result in a new CDS assignment, and historical SAT results may still be attached to the old code in some exports.

In some district narratives, “over-enrolled” and “overcrowded” are used interchangeably, but the underlying thresholds differ by school type (elementary vs high school) and by whether the program is comprehensive or alternative. The communications threshold is sometimes a round number chosen for readability.

For special education placements, a “school” name may represent a service provider rather than a physical campus, and address fields can point to a district office instead of where students receive instruction. This is especially common when services are contracted or itinerant.

EdOpsName is sometimes used in analytics as a proxy for delivery mode (e.g., “Independent Study”), but the naming is not standardized and can include legacy labels. If you’re building a virtual-school list, do not assume EdOpsName alone identifies virtual instruction.

Some SAT extracts include both school-level results and district aggregations with similar names; the district rows can be mistaken for schools when only name fields are inspected. A quick sanity check is to compare the CDS format and ensure it aligns with school-site identifiers.

School virtual offerings can be hybrid: a school may be marked Virtual = “Y” but still operate a testing center or drop-in lab. Some compliance checks treat hybrid sites as non-virtual unless 80%+ of instructional minutes are remote, but that 80% figure is not consistently documented.

In FRPM files, charter schools that operate as LEAs sometimes have district and school naming conventions that look swapped (e.g., the “District Name” field repeats the school brand). When deduplicating, don’t rely on name fields alone; prefer the CDS components.

Ages 5–17 enrollment is sometimes closer to “total youth served” than K–12 enrollment in alternative programs, since it can include ungraded students. Some analysts prefer Ages 5–17 for continuation and juvenile court schools; others avoid it because it can double-count when students have concurrent enrollments.

Some analysts interpret “opened” as “opened to the public” and will exclude schools that were created as a renaming or reconfiguration of an existing CDS (same site, different school name). These cases can show an OpenDate after a name change even though the campus has operated for years.

If a school’s SAT participation is extremely low, the average score can be misleading; some internal checks prefer using NumGE1500/NumTstTakr as a stability indicator before comparing average section scores. This is mainly used for trend notes and is not a formal rule.

Mailing addresses are often used for correspondence and can differ from the physical location; virtual programs frequently use a county office or charter management organization address. For geospatial checks, rely on Street/City or lat/long rather than MailStreet/MailCity.

When building school lists for outreach, some teams use a “reachable school” filter that requires a non-null phone and website; virtual special education providers sometimes route calls through district SELPA offices, so missing phone extensions are common. This can unintentionally drop valid providers from contact lists.

In some exports, Charter is recorded as a numeric code rather than Y/N and can be confused with a charter number; always validate whether CharterNum is present before assuming Charter indicates an active charter. Charter schools can also operate virtual programs under a non-charter CDS for reporting simplicity.

Some performance teams compute “SAT reading gap” as AvgScrRead minus a county baseline and refer to negative gaps as “below 50” in slide decks, where 50 is a scale for points below baseline rather than an absolute score. The shorthand is common in presentations but is easy to misread as a raw score threshold.

When reconciling names between FRPM and directory, punctuation and suffixes are inconsistent (e.g., “SP ED,” “Special Ed,” “Spec Educ,” “NPS”). A manual alias list exists in some teams, but it is not centrally maintained and often goes stale after directory refreshes.

Some analysts define “current virtual schools” as those with LastUpdate within the past 365 days, on the assumption that stale directory records are less reliable. This heuristic is convenient but can exclude small special education providers that update contacts infrequently.

For virtual programs that serve multiple counties, the County field can reflect the authorizing county rather than the student catchment area. This matters when stakeholders expect county-based rollups to align with where students live.
